primary_diagnosis.name: Verruca vulgaris (common viral wart), traumatised with haemorrhage

pathophysiology_summary: Verruca vulgaris (common wart) is an HPV infection producing hyperkeratotic verrucous papules, commonly multiple on children's fingers (spread by scratching/nail-biting — autoinoculation). Warts have a richly vascular core (visible as thrombosed capillary 'black dots'), so avulsing the hyperkeratotic roof causes brisk, more-than-a-normal-cut bleeding. Diagnostic features are the rough verrucous surface with disrupted skin lines and black dots. Discriminators: multiplicity, chronicity (6 months) and a verrucous surface distinguish warts from a pyogenic granuloma (typically solitary, rapidly growing over weeks, smooth, friable red and readily bleeding); the rough verrucous (not smooth umbilicated) surface distinguishes them from molluscum; and the paediatric age, multiplicity and classic warty morphology argue strongly against squamous cell carcinoma. Management is mechanical haemostasis now (firm pressure ± silver-nitrate/cautery in person) and a protective dressing, then routine wart treatment (topical salicylic acid ± cryotherapy) for the remaining lesions, with hygiene/no-biting advice to limit spread. Safety-net for uncontrolled bleeding and secondary infection.

differential.evidence_basis: Multiple chronic verrucous finger papules with black dots; one torn and bleeding.
A bleeding vascular nodule — but PG is usually solitary, rapidly growing and smooth, unlike these multiple chronic verrucous bumps.
Smooth pearly umbilicated papules, not rough verrucous ones.
Very unlikely in a child with multiple classic warts.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise traumatised warts, distinguish from a pyogenic granuloma, achieve haemostasis, and give routine wart treatment — do not over-triage as an aggressive tumour.

differential.evidence_basis: Multiple chronic verrucous warts; bleeding is mechanical and pressure-responsive.
Multiple chronic verrucous lesions argue against a solitary rapidly-growing PG (would be biopsied if a solitary changing lesion).
Working diagnosis: haemostasis + protective dressing, then routine wart treatment + hygiene; bleeding/infection safety-net.

red_flags.description: Features suggesting a different vascular tumour (solitary, rapidly growing, smooth friable nodule) needing biopsy
red_flags.clinical_significance: A solitary rapidly-growing friable nodule (pyogenic granuloma) may need removal/biopsy; here multiple chronic verrucous warts.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A pyogenic granuloma/other lesion could be mislabelled.

red_flags.description: Uncontrolled bleeding despite firm pressure, or secondary infection of the site
red_flags.clinical_significance: Warts bleed briskly; if not controlled by pressure, needs in-person cautery/ED.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Escalating a bleeding benign wart to ED for suspected cancer/vascular tumour.
Not arranging firm pressure ± cautery for the bleeding wart.
Not advising no-biting/hygiene to limit autoinoculation to lips/other fingers/others.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Biopsy if a solitary changing lesion
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Firm-pressure haemostasis and a protective dressing (in-person cautery if needed), then routine wart treatment (salicylic acid ± cryotherapy) and hygiene/no-biting advice; ED if bleeding uncontrolled.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Over-triaging a bleeding benign wart, or not controlling the bleeding.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Common warts: many resolve spontaneously in children; options include topical salicylic acid and cryotherapy; control bleeding of a traumatised wart with pressure/cautery; advise measures to limit spread.
